macro_segments.title: The Small and Large Courtyard Case: Resolving the Lechi Visibility Debate

micro_segments.title: Setting up the problem: Why doesn't the small courtyard's extending walls count as a lechi?
Rabbi's position: A chatzer requires two planks, three tefachim each, not just a lechi
The Gemara's challenge: Why specify exactly 10 and 11 amot if visibility from outside doesn't matter?
The Gemara's conclusion: The case proves that visible-from-outside-only counts as a lechi

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Forgotten Teaching: The Lechi Extending from the Mavoi Wall

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef claims he never heard Rabbi Huna's ruling that outside-visibility counts as a lechi
Abaye counters: You yourself told us the case of a lechi extending with the mavoi wall
The lechi-mashuch case: Less than four amot counts as a lechi; four amot or more is a mavoi wall
Three inferences from the lechi-mashuch teaching: Opposition to lechi, minimum mavoi size, and visibility rule
The Gemara's resolution: Despite the courtyard case contradicting it, we rule that outside-visibility counts as a lechi

macro_segments.title: The Opening Width Problem: Solving Mavoi Openings Wider Than Ten Amot

micro_segments.title: The mishna rules: An opening wider than ten amot must be made smaller
Rabbi Yehuda disputes: No need to make the opening smaller; he allows larger openings
The pasi biraot analogy: Wells in public spaces with walls of huge gaps; maximum 13⅓ amot
The Gemara rejects the qal vachomer: Pasi biraot is too lenient to serve as a model for the strict mavoi rules

macro_segments.title: The Dividing Wall Solution: Levi's Lengthwise Division of a Wide Mavoi

micro_segments.title: Levi's simple but rejected solution: Put a post in the middle of a 20-amah opening
Levi's accepted solution: A four-amah-deep plank lengthwise divides two separate mavois
Why lengthwise works: Air space on the sides doesn't negate it; the plank defines a wall, not a lechi

macro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's Solution: Strategic Gaps and Planks to Reduce the Opening

micro_segments.title: For a 15-amah opening: Two-amah gap, three-amah plank; the plank majority seals off five amot
The air-space negation principle: Too much open space on both sides can negate a central wall
Why not alternate small walls? The Gemara debates whether omed keparutz (equal to gap) works
The superior arrangement: 1.5-amah, 2-amah gap, 1.5-amah spreads out the walls more effectively

macro_segments.title: The Concern Over Side Doors and the Assumption About Human Behavior

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's worry: People might abandon the main door and exit through the side gap instead
Rav Adda's response: People don't abandon a large door for a small one—a strong presumption
Distinguishing from the broken wall case: Here the side 'door' is not a shortcut; it's all on the same face

macro_segments.title: The Leather Toilet Seat: Combining Closed and Open Space When Air Negates the Gap

micro_segments.title: The case: A leather toilet seat (tepach by tepach) over a dead body; when does the hole matter?
Rav Dimi's measure: Two-by-two-by-two (fingerbreadths); both sides and middle equal
Ravin's measure: 1.5-1-1.5; each side is bigger than the middle
Abaye's challenge: You do disagree—about whether you need each side individually bigger than the middle
The core dispute: Can you combine both sides to beat the middle, or must each side individually be bigger?
Rav Dimi's response: I gave an equal example, not a combining example, because I don't allow combining sides

topical_tags.term: Visible from outside, flush inside (nira mibachutz ve-shaveh mibifnim)
Lavud (joining by proximity within three tefachim)
Rabbi Yosi's three-tefachim requirement for planks
Rav Zeira and Ravina's special case (walls extending inward)
Four amot threshold (lechi becomes mavoi wall)
Thirteen and one-third amot (maximum pasi biraot opening)
Air negation (atya avira dihay gisa umevatel lei)
Omed meruba al haparutz (majority standing over opening)
Omed keparutz (standing equal to opening, disputed)
Shortcut concern (hamit locha vs. not a shortcut)
Mitztarfin (combining closed space from both sides)
